ideas.Live according to nature, not convention:
The injunction to "**live according to nature**" (*physis*) rather than **convention** (*nomos*) is the guiding principle of Diogenes of Sinope's philosophy and of the entire **Cynic** movement he came to embody. Diogenes held that the artificial customs, laws, manners, and values of human society are largely corrupt, arbitrary, and enslaving — obstacles to a good and free life — and that true virtue and happiness lie in stripping these away and living simply, in accordance with our genuine natural needs.

For Diogenes, most of what civilization piles upon us — wealth, status, reputation, luxury, elaborate social conventions, the pursuit of power and fame — is unnecessary and even harmful, breeding artificial desires that make us anxious, dependent, and unhappy. Nature, by contrast, requires very little: the satisfaction of our basic physical needs. The wise person recognizes this and rejects the tyranny of custom, embracing a life of extreme **simplicity** and self-sufficiency modeled on nature and, provocatively, on the **animals**, who live content with little and without shame. Famously, seeing a boy drinking water from his cupped hands, Diogenes is said to have thrown away his own cup, declaring the boy had bested him in simplicity.

Diogenes practiced this radical naturalism with theatrical rigor and deliberate provocation: he lived in poverty, reputedly making his home in a **large ceramic jar** (or "tub") in the marketplace, owning almost nothing, begging for food, and performing in public acts that flouted convention in order to expose its absurdity. His life was itself his philosophy — a living demonstration that one could be free and self-sufficient by needing almost nothing and caring nothing for society's opinion. This ideal of a life pared down to nature, in defiant rejection of corrupting convention, profoundly influenced **Stoicism** and remains the enduring core of Cynic thought.
ideas.Self-sufficiency (autarkeia):
**Self-sufficiency** (*autarkeia*) is one of the central values of Diogenes of Sinope and the Cynics — the ideal of being **complete and content in oneself**, dependent on nothing external for one's happiness and well-being. For Diogenes, the truly free and happy person is the one who has reduced their needs and desires to the barest minimum, so that they require almost nothing from the world, from other people, or from fortune, and thus cannot be controlled, threatened, or made unhappy by the loss of external things.

Diogenes reasoned that our dependence on externals — wealth, possessions, comforts, social status, the good opinion of others — is precisely the source of our anxiety, servility, and unhappiness. The more we need, the more vulnerable we are, and the more enslaved to the things and people we depend on. The path to freedom, therefore, is to **need less**: to train oneself, through deliberate austerity and hardship, to be satisfied with the simplest food, minimal clothing, and no fixed home, so that nothing external has power over us. Diogenes famously hardened himself to endure heat and cold, hunger and discomfort, embracing poverty as a discipline of liberation rather than a misfortune.

This ideal of *autarkeia* is closely tied to the Cynic notions of *askēsis* (rigorous training or self-discipline) and freedom (*eleutheria*). By his radical self-sufficiency, Diogenes claimed a kind of sovereign independence and inner freedom that even kings might envy — hence the famous (probably legendary) encounter in which Alexander the Great, offering to grant any wish, is told by Diogenes only to "stand out of my sunlight," the self-sufficient philosopher wanting nothing the mighty conqueror could give. This exaltation of self-sufficiency as the key to freedom and happiness passed directly into **Stoicism** and remains one of Cynicism's most influential legacies.
ideas.Shamelessness as philosophical practice:
"**Shamelessness**" (*anaideia*) — the deliberate, public flouting of social conventions, decency, and taboo — was a hallmark of Diogenes of Sinope's provocative philosophical practice, and one of the most distinctive (and notorious) features of Cynicism. Far from mere crude exhibitionism, Diogenes's shamelessness was a calculated philosophical **strategy**: by openly performing acts that society deemed shameful, he aimed to expose the arbitrariness of social convention and to demonstrate his own freedom from its tyranny.

Diogenes's reasoning followed from his commitment to living according to **nature** rather than **convention**. If an act is natural and not genuinely harmful, he held, then there is no real reason to be ashamed of it or to hide it — the shame we feel is merely a product of arbitrary social conditioning (*nomos*), not of nature. To perform such "shameful" natural acts openly and without embarrassment is therefore both a demonstration of one's liberation from convention and a pointed challenge to the audience, forcing them to confront the artificiality of their own inhibitions. He reportedly ate, and performed other bodily functions, in the public marketplace, defending himself by pointing out that these acts are natural and that if they are not wrong to do, they are not wrong to do in public.

The very name "**Cynic**" (*kynikos*, "dog-like") reflects this stance: Diogenes was called "**the Dog**" for living shamelessly like an animal — eating, sleeping, and relieving himself in public, indifferent to human proprieties — and he embraced the label. Like his other provocations, this "dog-like" shamelessness was a form of philosophical *performance*, a living argument meant to shock people out of their complacent acceptance of convention and to point toward a more natural, honest, and free way of life. Confrontational and deliberately outrageous, it made Diogenes an unforgettable figure and a model of philosophy lived as radical practice.
ideas.Cosmopolitanism:
**Cosmopolitanism** — the idea of being a "**citizen of the world**" — is one of Diogenes of Sinope's most important and enduring contributions, and quite possibly his coinage. When asked where he came from, Diogenes is famously reported to have replied, "I am a **kosmopolitēs**" — a citizen of the *kosmos*, the world — rather than of any particular city-state. In an age when personal identity and moral obligation were bound up almost entirely with membership in one's *polis*, this was a radical and revolutionary declaration.

Diogenes's cosmopolitanism flows directly from his Cynic rejection of **convention** in favor of **nature**. The boundaries between city-states, the laws and loyalties that divide people into Athenians, Corinthians, or Sinopeans, are for Diogenes merely artificial human conventions (*nomos*), not grounded in nature. What truly matters is not one's accidental civic origin but one's common **humanity** and one's relationship to the natural order and to reason, which are shared by all people everywhere. The wise person therefore refuses to be confined by the parochial loyalties and prejudices of a particular city, recognizing instead a kinship with all human beings and belonging to the world as a whole.

For Diogenes, a Cynic in permanent voluntary exile who owned nothing and belonged nowhere, this cosmic citizenship was also an expression of his radical **freedom** and self-sufficiency: the man who is at home everywhere and beholden to no particular state is bound by none of its constraints. This idea proved immensely influential. It was taken up and developed by the **Stoics** into a rich philosophy of universal human community and natural law binding all rational beings, and through them it flowed into later conceptions of universal human rights, world citizenship, and the moral unity of humankind — making Diogenes's offhand reply one of the most consequential ideas in the history of ethics and political thought.

works.No surviving writings; anecdotes in Diog:
Diogenes of Sinope — "**the Dog**," founder in practice of the **Cynic** way of life — left behind **no surviving writings**, and it is uncertain whether he composed any lasting works at all (some ancient sources credited him with dialogues and a *Republic*, but these are lost and their authenticity was disputed even in antiquity). This is fitting, for Diogenes's philosophy was emphatically one of **action and example** rather than doctrine and text: he "wrote" his philosophy in the way he *lived*, through his deeds, gestures, and pointed provocations, not in treatises. His life *was* his argument.

Consequently, our knowledge of Diogenes comes almost entirely through the **anecdotes** (*chreiai*) that accumulated around him — pithy, memorable stories, sayings, and witty ripostes preserved and retold by later authors. By far the most important source is **Diogenes Laërtius**, whose *Lives of the Eminent Philosophers* (third century CE) devotes a rich chapter to him, gathering the many colorful tales that made him legendary: living in a **jar** (or tub) in the marketplace; carrying a **lamp in daylight** while "looking for an honest man"; telling **Alexander the Great** to "stand out of my sunlight"; throwing away his cup on seeing a boy drink from his hands; being sold into slavery and declaring his trade was "governing men"; and countless barbed exchanges exposing hypocrisy and convention.

Whether or not every anecdote is historically accurate — and many are surely embellished or invented — this body of story and saying is precisely the appropriate vehicle for a philosophy that scorned abstract theory in favor of a vividly enacted way of life. The *chreiai* transmit not a system of propositions but a **character** and an ethical stance: radical simplicity, self-sufficiency, shameless honesty, and defiant freedom. Through these anecdotes, the living example of Diogenes exerted enormous influence on the Cynic and Stoic traditions and on the Western imagination.
